title: Dog Brown Ear Discharge and Head Shaking: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog shaking their head with brown ear discharge? Learn about potential causes like yeast infections and when you need to visit a vet.
content_html:
What is brown discharge and head shaking?

When a dog exhibits brown, waxy, or crumbly discharge accompanied by persistent head shaking, it is usually a sign of an ear infection, known as otitis externa. This discharge is often a buildup of yeast, bacteria, or ear wax caused by inflammation in the ear canal. The head shaking is a reflex to alleviate the itching, pressure, or pain deep inside the ear.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for brown ear discharge and head shaking is generally Low. While it is uncomfortable for your pet and can be painful, it is not typically a life-threatening situation. However, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can lead to more severe issues, such as a ruptured eardrum or an aural hematoma from excessive shaking.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the inside of your dog's ear flap and the discharge itself is extremely helpful for triage. It allows a professional to observe the color, consistency, and volume of the debris, as well as the degree of redness or swelling. This information helps determine whether the issue looks like a standard yeast overgrowth or something that requires more immediate diagnostic testing.

faqs.question: Can I use human ear drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter drops without a vet's approval, as some ingredients can be toxic to dogs or damaging if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: Is brown ear discharge always ear mites?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While ear mites cause dark discharge, yeast and bacterial infections are much more common causes in adult dogs.

faqs.question: How can I make my dog comfortable until the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog from scratching at the ear by using an Elizabethan collar (cone) and avoid putting any liquids or cleaners into the ear until the vet has performed an exam.
